description: Perform a Blanchard-Quah (1989) structural VAR decomposition on US macroeconomic data to identify supply and demand shocks using gretl's VAR estimation and matrix algebra.

natural_language.prompt:
Using usa.gdt (quarterly US data, 1984:1-2009:3), perform a Blanchard-Quah (1989) structural VAR decomposition to identify supply and demand shocks in the US economy.

Create GDP growth as the annualized log-difference of GDP (gdp_growth = 100 * diff(log(gdp))). Confirm both gdp_growth and inf are stationary via ADF tests (4 lags).

Select the VAR lag order that minimizes AIC (test lags 1-8) and estimate the reduced-form VAR with the ordering (gdp_growth, inf).

Apply the Blanchard-Quah long-run identification: the second structural shock (demand) must have zero cumulative long-run effect on GDP growth. Compute and report the structural impact matrix and the long-run impact matrix, verifying the zero restriction holds.

Save a reproducible Gretl script to bq_svar.inp and all numerical output (structural matrices, ADF results) to bq_results.txt in /home/ga/Documents/gretl_output/.
natural_language.context_hint: After estimating a VAR in gretl, the companion matrix is available as $compan and the residual covariance as $sigma. The companion matrix for a VAR(p) with n variables is (np x np); the lag coefficient matrices A_i are in rows 1:n, columns ((i-1)*n+1):(i*n). Use the hansl matrix functions inv(), cholesky(), and standard matrix arithmetic.

metadata.long_run_restriction: demand shock has zero cumulative effect on gdp_growth
